title: Listening - Part 3: The Ghost in the Machine
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with an art critic, Abbi, and a computer scientist, Elliot, about whether AI can be truly creative. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview exploring the definition of art, the role of intent, and the future of human artists.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: Can an algorithm feel 'inspiration'? With AI-generated paintings winning awards and AI-composed music topping charts, we are facing a crisis of definitions. I’m joined by Abbi, an art critic, and Elliot, a researcher in generative AI. Abbi, is a machine-made image 'art'?

Abbi: [15] It depends on how you define 'art.' If art is just a pleasing arrangement of pixels or notes, then yes. But for me, art requires 'intent.' It’s a communication between two souls. [/15] An AI doesn't have a childhood, it doesn't have heartbreaks, and it doesn't have a point of view. It’s just a statistical mirror of everything humans have already created. It can mimic style, but it can't invent a new way of seeing the world.

Interviewer: Elliot, how do you respond to that? Is the AI just a sophisticated photocopier?

Elliot: [16] That’s a bit unfair. Human artists are also 'trained' on everything they’ve seen and heard. No artist works in a vacuum. What the AI does is 'combinatorial creativity.' [/16] It can find connections between disparate styles—like mixing Van Gogh with a 1980s computer game—in ways a human brain might never consider. The 'intent' comes from the person writing the prompt. The AI is a new kind of brush, not a replacement for the artist.

Interviewer: Abbi, we’re seeing 'human-made' labels starting to appear on books and music. Is this the future?

Abbi: [17] It’s a sign of desperation. We are terrified that we won't be able to tell the difference. If a machine can produce a perfect pop song in three seconds, the value of human effort starts to evaporate. [/17] We are moving toward a world where 'imperfection' becomes the hallmark of quality. We will value a painting *because* it has a visible mistake, as that mistake is proof of a human hand.

Interviewer: Elliot, what about the copyright issue? Is the AI 'stealing' from human artists?

Elliot: [18] It’s a legal 'grey zone.' The AI isn't storing copies of the art; it’s learning the 'math' behind the style. However, I agree that the original creators should be compensated or at least have the right to 'opt out' of the training data. [/18] We need a new social contract between tech companies and the creative community. Without human artists to learn from, the AI will eventually start 'inbreeding'—learning from its own output—and the quality will collapse.

Interviewer: Abbi, will the role of the 'artist' change?

Abbi: [19] The artist will become a 'curator.' Instead of spending years learning to draw a perfect circle, they will spend their time exploring concepts and selecting the best outputs from the machine. [/19] But I worry we will lose the 'struggle.' The struggle with the medium is often what produces the most profound art. If it’s too easy, will it still mean anything?

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Elliot? Is there a limit to what AI can do?

Elliot: [20] AI can't understand 'context.' It can paint a beautiful sunset, but it doesn't know why a sunset makes a human feel sad or hopeful. It knows the 'syntax' of art, but not the 'semantics.' That’s where the human will always be essential. [/20]

Interviewer: Abbi, Elliot, thank you.

questions.question: Abbi argues that true art must involve
options: A) a high level of technical skill.
B) the use of traditional materials like paint or clay.
C) a conscious message or purpose from the creator.
D) a style that has never been seen before.
questions.explanation: She states that art requires 'intent' and is a 'communication between two souls', something AI lacks.

questions.question: What is Elliot's view of how AI creates?
options: A) It is completely original and does not rely on human work.
B) It follows the same learning process as a human artist.
C) It is unable to combine different styles effectively.
D) It will eventually make human artists obsolete.
questions.explanation: He argues that human artists are also 'trained' on what they see and hear, and that AI uses 'combinatorial creativity'.

questions.question: Abbi suggests that 'imperfections' in art will become more valuable because
options: A) they make the art cheaper to produce.
B) they are more visually interesting than perfection.
C) they serve as evidence that a human created the work.
D) machines are becoming too expensive to maintain.
questions.explanation: She says we will value a visible mistake as 'proof of a human hand' in a world of perfect machine-made content.

questions.question: Regarding training data, Elliot warns that if human artists stop contributing
options: A) the AI will become too powerful to control.
B) the quality of AI-generated art will decline.
C) tech companies will go bankrupt.
D) art will become too expensive for the public.
questions.explanation: He mentions 'inbreeding', where AI learning from its own output causes quality to 'collapse'.

questions.question: Abbi is concerned that the future of being an artist
options: A) will require too much knowledge of computer science.
B) will be less meaningful because the 'struggle' is removed.
C) will be restricted to those who can afford the best software.
D) will result in too much art being produced for the market.
questions.explanation: She worries about losing the 'struggle' with the medium, which she believes produces profound art.

questions.question: According to Elliot, what is the main thing AI cannot do?
options: A) paint a realistic image of a natural scene
B) follow the rules of musical composition
C) understand the emotional meaning behind an image
D) create art as quickly as a human can.
questions.explanation: He says AI doesn't know why a sunset makes a human feel sad or hopeful; it knows 'syntax' but not 'semantics'.
